prose:
## Fukui Reactivity Analysis — Pyridine

---

### Method Provenance Block

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Property** | Fukui reactivity indices (f⁺, f⁻, f⁰, dual descriptor) via finite-difference Mulliken population |
| **Level of theory** | DFT, tier `standard` → **B3LYP / def2-TZVP** (PySCF RKS) |
| **Density fitting** | OFF (exact four-center two-electron integrals; no RI approximation) |
| **Geometry provenance** | User-supplied `.xyz` at `/home/erobles/chem-skills/benchmarks/fidelity/fukui-reactivity-validation/pyridine/pyridine.xyz`; used as-is (single-point, not re-optimized) |
| **Solvent / environment** | **Gas phase** (no `--solvent` flag; null in result JSON) |
| **Charge / multiplicity** | 0 / 1 (neutral, closed-shell singlet) |
| **Cation (N−1) state** | charge +1, multiplicity 2 (doublet) |
| **Anion (N+1) state** | charge −1, multiplicity 2 (doublet) |
| **Partial-charge scheme** | Mulliken (PySCF) |
| **Software** | PySCF (chemkit DFT engine) |
| **Convergence** | All three SCF calculations (neutral, cation, anion) converged; integrity status = `ok`, `trustworthy = true` |
| **Warnings** | **None** — no warnings reported by the engine |

**Integrity checks (all passed):**
- `fukui_charge_conservation_f+`: sum(f⁺) = 1.0000 ✅
- `fukui_charge_conservation_f-`: sum(f⁻) = 1.0000 ✅
- `schema_header`: common header present ✅

---

### Fukui Index Results (per atom, 0-based index)

Pyridine has 11 atoms: **N(1), C(2), C(3), C(4), C(5), C(6), H(7), H(8), H(9), H(10), H(11)**

| Atom | Symbol | f⁺ (electrophilic attack) | f⁻ (nucleophilic attack) | f⁰ (radical) | Dual Descriptor |
|------|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **N** | **0.48423** | 0.06412 | 0.27417 | **+0.42010** |
| 2 | C | 0.32447 | 0.00619 | 0.16533 | +0.31828 |
| 3 | C | 0.14032 | 0.11823 | 0.12928 | +0.02209 |
| 4 | C | 0.14040 | **0.11826** | 0.12933 | +0.02213 |
| 5 | C | 0.29963 | 0.11207 | 0.20585 | +0.18756 |
| 6 | C | 0.29948 | 0.11201 | 0.20574 | +0.18747 |
| 7 | H | −0.14151 | 0.08285 | −0.02933 | −0.22436 |
| 8 | H | −0.13368 | 0.09818 | −0.01775 | −0.23186 |
| 9 | H | −0.13366 | 0.09818 | −0.01774 | −0.23184 |
| 10 | H | −0.13982 | 0.09496 | −0.02243 | −0.23478 |
| 11 | H | −0.13985 | 0.09495 | −0.02245 | −0.23479 |

---

### Summary of Key Reactivity Sites

**Most electrophilic site (highest f⁺, attacked by nucleophiles):**
> **Atom 1 — N (nitrogen)**, f⁺ = **0.48422572**
> The nitrogen atom is the dominant site for nucleophilic *attack on the electron-deficient ring* and is also the preferred site for electrophilic addition to the lone pair.

**Most nucleophilic site (highest f⁻, attacked by electrophiles):**
> **Atom 4 — C (carbon, position 4)**, f⁻ = **0.11826260**
> This β-carbon (para to N) is the most susceptible to electrophilic aromatic substitution, consistent with pyridine's known reactivity pattern where the 4-position is preferred over 2/3 positions under SEAr conditions.

**Dual descriptor interpretation:**
> Positive dual descriptor (f⁺ − f⁻ > 0) on N and C2/C5/C6 → **electrophilic character** at those sites (susceptible to nucleophilic attack). Negative dual descriptor on all H atoms → **nucleophilic character** at H positions (low chemical consequence). The strong positive dual at N (0.42010) confirms nitrogen as the dominant LUMO-driven reactive center.

---

### Reproducibility

- **CLI invocation:** `/home/erobles/chem-skills/mcp_server/chemkit_engine/cli.py fukui /home/erobles/chem-skills/benchmarks/fidelity/fukui-reactivity-validation/pyridine/pyridine.xyz --method dft --tier standard --functional b3lyp --basis def2-tzvp --charge 0 --mult 1 --stdout path --out pyridine_fukui_dft.json`
- **Output JSON:** `pyridine_fukui_dft.json`
- **Bar chart plot:** `/home/erobles/chem-skills/benchmarks/fidelity/fukui-reactivity-validation/pyridine/argo_claude-sonnet-4.6/20260704-153129_pyridine_fukui_reactivity/agent_call_01_plot.png`

---

### References

- Sun, Q.; et al. Recent Developments in the PySCF Program Package. *J. Chem. Phys.* **2020**, *153*, 024109. https://doi.org/10.1063/5.0006074
- Parr, R. G.; Yang, W. Density Functional Approach to the Frontier-Electron Theory of Chemical Reactivity. *J. Am. Chem. Soc.* **1984**, *106*, 4049–4050. https://doi.org/10.1021/ja00326a036
